danger_signals.visual_form: Large sprite-based creatures moving across screen
Lava, water, falling debris in level design
danger_signals.meaning: Passive world threats independent of faction

danger_signals.visual_form: Rapid movement speed increase, directional charging behavior
danger_signals.meaning: Dinosaur has detected player; attack imminent

safety_signals.visual_form: Item sprites (likely projectile weapons or tools)
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary damage capability or utility enhancement
Recovery point; sanctuary in immediate vicinity

safety_signals.visual_form: Doors, exits, or boundary markers in platformer layout
safety_signals.meaning: Safe progression; escape from current threat area

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Lab facilities, control rooms, safe structures implied by platformer progression

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple dinosaur species as distinct sprite sets, varying size/behavior
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Jungle environments, prehistoric terrain, nesting areas
Facility doors, corridors, technological architecture in level design

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Structured facility layouts, organized platformer pathways, controlled environments
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Dinosaurs remain in containment areas; player navigates through infrastructure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Increased dinosaur density on-screen, environmental decay suggested by hazards, broken barriers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Dinosaurs roaming freely; aggressive AI behavior increases; survival-focused gameplay

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: High sprite density, fast-moving predators, environmental instability
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Rapid enemy aggression, need for evasion or combat; player forced into reaction-based platforming

environmental_storytelling.example: Level design moves from controlled facility (lab/corridors) to increasingly wild jungle environments, implying loss of infrastructure control
Predators appear more frequently in later levels, visually communicating escalating threat and ecosystem dominance over human space
Mix of technological (electrical, mechanical) and natural (lava, water) obstacles suggests hybrid world where nature reclaims technology

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Slow movement, standard patrol paths; visual cue of non-alert state
Speed increase, directional charge toward player position; visual acceleration in sprite animation
Rapid bouncing/darting patterns; harder to predict trajectory
Non-hostile movement; visual distinction from predator sprites suggests different threat level

notes: Source provided is a Wikipedia disambiguation page with minimal technical detail about the SNES version's visual design. Extraction is based on inferential understanding of 1993 SNES platformer conventions and Jurassic Park thematic elements. Actual sprite designs, color palettes, and detailed behavioral animations cannot be verified from available source. Game likely uses contrasting sprite sizes and movement speeds to signal threat levels. Faction identity is primarily visual differentiation between human player character and multiple dinosaur species rather than traditional political/team affiliations.
